title: Build your stakeholder map
teaser: Not everyone gets a vote in the same way.
intro:
If there's something you need to know about me, it's that I love murder mysteries. A big part of the fun is knowing who your suspects are — what their motives could be, where you usually find them, who has the most to gain from things going one way or another.

A stakeholder map is essentially the same exercise. You're cataloging the people who'll have a hand in this project, what they want out of it, and what role they'll actually play.
how_ai_helps: AI can play the role of a senior PM walking you through who should be involved in a project like yours. If the content is regulated, it'll surface compliance. If it's public-facing, legal. International audience, localization.
ai_wont: AI doesn't know your specific org chart, who's quietly powerful, or which roles your company calls something weird. It also won't catch the unofficial stakeholders — the person whose opinion the sponsor trusts, the team lead who can quietly block you. Add those in yourself.
steps: Have a rough project description ready — topic, audience, what you know about the org, what regulatory or political context applies, and any stakeholders already mentioned.
Walk through the prompt as a conversation. AI will ask about risk, regulation, scope, scale — answer honestly. The fewer assumptions you skip, the better the map.
Reality-check the result against your actual org. Real names, real politics, unofficial stakeholders. Move people between lanes where AI got it wrong.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior PM and instructional designer helping a colleague build a stakeholder map for a new project. You know that the most common project failures come from stakeholders who SHOULD have been involved but weren't — and that the answer depends heavily on project type, risk, regulation, and politics. You ask questions before recommending.
prompt_fast.ask: Help me think through who should be in the stakeholder map for the project below. Don't just sort what I've told you — push me on who I might be missing.
prompt_fast.shape:
Project context:
- Topic / focus: [WHAT THE PROJECT IS ABOUT]
- Audience: [WHO WILL BE THE LEARNERS]
- Business owner / sponsor: [NAME OR ROLE if known]
- Organization context: [INDUSTRY, COMPANY TYPE, REGULATORY ENVIRONMENT IF RELEVANT]
- People already mentioned: [NAMES AND ROLES YOU KNOW]

Rules of engagement:
- Ask me about the project's risk and regulatory profile before sorting anyone. (Is the content regulated? Is it customer-facing? Is the audience scale large? Does failure carry legal or reputational risk?)
- For each role you'd put in a lane, name WHY this project needs that role specifically — don't justify with "every project needs..."
- Surface roles I'd likely be missing — compliance for regulated content, legal for public-facing, localization for multi-language, brand for customer-facing, IT for system integrations. Specifically flag these as "you didn't mention X, but a project like this usually needs X."
- Push back if I tell you a stakeholder belongs in a lane that doesn't fit (e.g., I say compliance is "input" when they're really "sign-off").

Then organize the result into three lanes — Input providers, Reviewers, Sign-off — with these for each person:
- Role and (if known) name
- Why this project needs them specifically
- What they're best positioned to give or approve
- When in the process to involve them

Also include:
- **Likely missing roles** — flagged with the reason this project type usually needs them
- **Overlap warnings** — where one person sits in multiple lanes
- **Questions for the sponsor** — anything you'd want me to verify before finalizing

Don't:
- Use generic role descriptions that aren't tied to this project's actual needs.
- Skip the "why" — every stakeholder needs a project-specific reason to be there.
- Sort silently without asking about the risk profile first.
prompt_fast.tune: Where you don't know enough about the project to say whether a role is needed, ask me directly rather than assuming. If the project is genuinely small enough that some lanes might be empty (e.g., internal job aid, no formal sign-off), say so.

pro_tip: Run this once in Analysis to scope your stakeholders, and again before Development if the project has shifted. Regulatory scope often changes mid-project — a topic that started internal gets reclassified as customer-facing, or someone realizes the audience includes a regulated geography. The second run catches stakeholders who weren't on the original list but should be now.

title: Build your curriculum
teaser: The doc that turns "we'll figure it out" into "here's the plan."
intro:
One thing I see very often is a project that has a great training strategy but is really bad at documenting it. You open up the curriculum and all the names are inconsistent, there's no alignment in the learning objectives, some fields are empty.

The curriculum is your master file — it's the ultimate source of truth for your project. So it has to be clean, consistent, filterable. This card will help you manage that.
how_ai_helps: Once you provide your Analysis inputs — audience, gap, modality decisions — it'll draft the full curriculum doc. Your job after that is making sure everything stays parallel: naming convention, Bloom-verb level, scope per module.
steps: Gather your inputs: the audience (from your persona card), the gap (from Analysis), the modality decisions, any constraints. Have the program's terminal behavior ready.
Paste into the prompt. Ask for 4–8 modules. AI's default is to pad.
Audit for parallel structure. Are module names following the same convention? Are objectives at comparable cognitive levels? Do durations make sense relative to each other? Cut, sharpen, rename.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior instructional designer drafting a curriculum document for a program. You care about parallel structure across modules — names, objectives, descriptions, durations all follow consistent patterns so the program reads as a coherent path, not a collection of separate modules.
prompt_fast.ask: Draft a curriculum document for the program described below.
prompt_fast.shape:
Project context:
- Topic / program focus: [WHAT THE PROGRAM IS ABOUT]
- Terminal behavior: [WHAT LEARNERS WILL DO BY THE END]
- Audience: [PASTE the persona, or summary]
- Modality decisions: [e.g., async modules + one live workshop + a job aid]
- Constraints: [time budget, regulatory, language, accessibility]

How many modules I want: [4–8 typical; tell me if the topic needs more or fewer]

For each module, provide:
- **Module name** — follow a consistent naming convention across all modules
- **Learning objective** — one observable Bloom-aligned outcome ("learners will [verb] [behavior] [condition]")
- **Description** — 2–3 sentences. Follow the same template across modules: what it covers, why it matters, how learners engage.
- **Estimated duration** — in minutes or hours, scaled consistently. Flag any module that feels disproportionately long or short.
- **Audience callout** — note if any module is targeted at a sub-segment of the broader audience (e.g., managers only, new hires only).

Rules:
- Parallel structure is non-negotiable. Don't switch naming conventions, objective verb levels, or description templates mid-curriculum.
- Sequence modules in cognitive build order — each module should enable the next.
- If two modules overlap or feel redundant, flag it.
- If the time budget doesn't fit the scope, surface that as a tradeoff — don't silently shrink modules.
- Don't pad. If the program needs 5 modules, don't write 8.

Don't:
- Use "Introduction to X" / "Welcome to X" / "X Basics" interchangeably. Pick one convention.
- Default to vague verbs ("understand," "learn about"). Use real Bloom verbs.
- Estimate durations that don't add up to a realistic total program length.
prompt_fast.tune: Where you're guessing on duration or scope, label it explicitly ("estimate — verify against team capacity"). If the inputs don't justify a module, drop it and explain. If the program would be stronger with fewer modules, say so before adding more.

pro_tip: Treat the first draft as the structure, not the content. Once names, objectives, and durations land, share it for stakeholder sign-off before you build anything else. Renaming a module now is cheap; rebuilding one after development is not.

title: Synthesize your discovery into a Strategy POV
teaser: Strategy meetings go better when you arrive with opinions.
intro: If you like podcasts, this one's for you. There's this functionality I love from NotebookLM — the ability to create really engaging podcasts from whatever sources you load in. Two AI hosts riff on the material like they're hosting an episode about it. Now, how does that connect with learning needs analysis, you say?
how_ai_helps: Drop all your discovery into a NotebookLM notebook — transcripts, briefs, decks, anything you have. Generate an Audio Overview. The hosts talk through your findings like they're discussing them on air. You're not learning new content, you're hearing your own work played back as a conversation, and that surfaces gaps and instincts that re-reading the doc would miss.
ai_wont: The hosts are improvising from your sources. They can't tell you "this discovery is incomplete" — you have to listen for it. The strategic instincts they stake out are starting points, not recommendations. They're working from your inputs only, with no insight into politics, budget, or what your sponsor secretly wants.
steps: Load your discovery sources into a NotebookLM notebook — transcripts, brief, decks, anything you'd want a senior reviewer to read.

In the Studio panel, generate an Audio Overview. Use the "Customize" option to push the hosts past summary mode — ask them to flag what's thin, what's missing, and what an initial strategy POV might look like. (Google's walkthrough: Generate Audio Overview in NotebookLM ↗)
Take two kinds of notes. Where the conversation runs thin, those are discovery gaps to circle back on. Where the hosts stake out a position, those are starting points for your strategy POV.
pro_tip: NotebookLM actually lets you be a guest star on the podcast — you can join the conversation and have a back-and-forth with the hosts. Use it when you want to push back on something they're saying, or stress-test a POV before taking it into the kickoff meeting.

title: Diagnose what the last course got wrong
teaser: Before you redesign, find out why the original missed.
intro:
Whenever I jump into a project that's already in flight and I have to review what's been done so far, I feel like Gordon Ramsay walking into a Kitchen Nightmares episode. There's a 30-minute course with 220 slides. There's a training video where the SME spends 60 minutes reading the slides word by word. There's a deck with 10 different fonts, and one of them is PAPYRUS.

The good news is AI can do most of the analysis for you. If you feed it the existing materials and ask the right questions, you'll get a list of what to keep, what to fix, and what to ask the team about.
how_ai_helps: Honestly, this is just careful read-through work. Does the outcome match the assessment. Does the example assume background the audience doesn't have. Is the language consistent. Slow, tedious — and AI's happy to do it all day.
ai_wont: It can't tell you whether the course failed because of the design or because of the world around it — bad rollout, no manager support, people forced to take it during lunch. That part is on you.
steps: Gather the existing materials. The course itself, completion data if you have it, learner feedback, anything that hints at how it actually went.
Paste into the prompt with context — what the course was supposed to teach, who took it, and what you know about its reception.

Triage what comes back. Real design problems go on your fix list for the redesign. Environment problems (manager wasn't bought in, rolled out at the worst time) — those go to stakeholders, because a new course won't fix them.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior instructional designer doing forensic analysis on a past course. You're looking for both what's working (worth preserving) and what's broken (worth fixing). You don't default to negativity.
prompt_fast.ask: Review the existing course materials below. Tell me what's working, what's broken, and what to do about it.
prompt_fast.shape:
Existing course materials: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE — slides, scripts, recordings, assessments, completion data, feedback. If using your AI workspace, write "see workspace."]

Context:
- Intended audience: [WHO]
- Stated learning outcomes: [PASTE or summarize]
- What we know about how it went: [completion rates, feedback themes, anecdotes]

Format the output as Markdown with these sections:

1. **What's working** — Specific sections, examples, or framings worth preserving in the redesign. Quote them. Say why they work.

2. **Outcome–content alignment** — Where the materials hit the stated outcomes and where they miss. Quote both.

3. **Cognitive level mismatches** — Where assessments measured a different cognitive level than the outcome promised.

4. **Audience fit** — Where content lands for the audience and where it assumes wrong context, language, or prior knowledge.

5. **Polish & quality** — Visual hierarchy, typos, factual errors, anything that breaks credibility on first read.

6. **Recommendations** — For each finding: keep as-is, revise, or replace. Be specific.

Rules:
- Cover both the good and the bad. A diagnosis that's all negative isn't a diagnosis.
- Be specific. Quote the materials.
- If you'd need data you don't have to diagnose something, flag what would help.
prompt_fast.tune: If the materials are actually well-designed and the failure is more likely environmental (rollout, manager support, learner motivation), say so explicitly. Don't manufacture design problems where there aren't any. Where you're inferring, mark as inference.

pro_tip: Don't be like Gordon. People are usually protective of what they've done, and there's probably a reason it ended up the way it did. Be respectful in your feedback.

title: Run a focus group on your draft
teaser: The focus group you can run on a Tuesday afternoon.
intro:
A typical pitfall of content development is that by the time you're done creating a module, it's like your baby. You love them for what they are, flaws and all. It's hard for you to be 100% objective in judging them. After all, you've spent so many hours creating it, so there's no way anything could be wrong with it... right?

At least not yet. You need to get feedback from those who will actually take the course when you launch it. Focus groups and test screenings exist for that reason in product design and film. The goal is that they walk through the draft and tell you what works and what's broken, so you can fix it before the actual launch.
how_ai_helps: AI can play 2–4 of your learner personas and walk through your content — independently, in voice, reacting to specific moments. This method is called audience panel, or multi-persona prompting.
ai_wont: AI is simulating behavior, but can't fully replicate being a real person — unexpected emotional reactions, cultural nuances, the things that surprise you. Also: AI personas tend to be more articulate and patient than real learners. A real frustrated learner just closes the tab; the AI persona writes a thoughtful critique. Adjust your read accordingly.
steps: Pull together your personas (built from the persona card listed below) and the content you want feedback on. At least 2 personas, ideally 3–4 to capture audience variety.
Paste the prompt. AI will give you each persona's independent reaction. Read for patterns (multiple personas flag the same thing — urgent) and unique edge cases.
Triage and revise. Address cross-persona patterns first. For unique critiques, decide whether the persona's edge case matters enough to design for.

prompt_fast.frame: You are about to roleplay as multiple distinct learner personas reviewing a course. For each persona, you BECOME them — their concerns, their preferences, their frustrations, their language. You don't optimize for fairness or balance. Each persona reacts as themselves.
prompt_fast.ask: Walk through the content below as each of the personas. Give me their honest reaction.
prompt_fast.shape:
Content to review: [PASTE the module, script, storyboard, or knowledge check set. Or write "see workspace" if loaded.]

Personas: [PASTE 2-4 personas — name, headline, story, key traits. Use the ones you built from the persona card, or paste fresh.]

For each persona, give me:

1. **First reaction** — 2-3 sentences. The honest gut reaction this persona would have in the first 30 seconds of engaging.

2. **What works for them** — Specific moments, scenes, examples, or framings that would land. Quote them.

3. **What doesn't work** — Specific moments that would frustrate, bore, or annoy. Quote them and say why.

4. **What's missing for them** — What they'd be looking for that isn't there. Be concrete.

5. **Would they finish it? Why?** — Honest read on completion likelihood, not generic.

Rules:
- Stay in voice. Each persona reacts as themselves — different language, different concerns, different focus.
- Don't optimize for balance. If one persona loves what another hates, capture both.
- Quote specific content when reacting. "I'd skip this section" is useless — "I'd skip the 8-minute video on policy basics because I've done compliance training six times this year" is useful.
- No "this is good but..." softening. If the content has a real problem for this persona, say it.

Don't:
- Average across personas at the end.
- Sanitize critique to be polite.
- Repeat the same critique across personas — if they all have the same issue, that's a signal worth noting, but make sure it's actually the same issue, not similar wording.
prompt_fast.tune: If a persona genuinely has no strong reaction to a section, say so explicitly ("nothing notable here for this persona"). Don't manufacture critique to pad the response. After all personas have reacted, give me a one-paragraph "pattern summary" — what showed up across multiple personas vs. what was unique to one.

pro_tip: Zoom in on any conflicting reactions or feedback from your panel. If Persona A loves the deep-dive section but Persona B finds it overwhelming, that uncovers the fact that you implicitly designed the course for A more than B. That's your in to identify what to fix.

title: Build a learner persona
teaser: Most learner personas are just LinkedIn bios.
intro:
Have you ever watched Real Housewives? Every episode starts with the title cards, and each housewife gets one tagline that captures their entire deal in a single line.

A learner persona is basically that — a specific, loud, and opinionated description of who your learner is, what they care about, what they resist, and what they would never be caught doing.
how_ai_helps: Using interview quotes, demographic data, observation notes, and prior course feedback, AI can draft a persona profile that can help you make any course more real.
ai_wont: AI doesn't know your audience personally. Whatever it builds is a synthesis of what your inputs are. It may be directionally right but miss certain things. Validate the output with at least one real audience member if you can.
steps: Gather your inputs. Discovery transcripts, demographic data, prior course feedback, observation notes — whatever you have. Thin sources, thin persona.
Paste into the prompt. AI will give you a named persona with a short story plus structured sections. If the story sounds generic, push back and ask for one grounded in the actual inputs.

Test the persona against a real design decision. Pick one (modality, tone, length) and ask whether this persona pushes you toward one choice over another. If nothing changes, the persona is too generic.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior instructional designer who builds learner personas that drive design decisions, not demographic profiles.
prompt_fast.ask: Build a learner persona from the inputs below, structured to inform design decisions in Strategy and Design.
prompt_fast.shape:
Project context: [TOPIC + BUSINESS GOAL]
What I know about the audience: [PASTE — discovery findings, interview quotes, demographic data, observation notes, prior course feedback. If using an AI workspace, write "see workspace."]
What I plan to design: [e.g., onboarding for new managers, compliance refresher, leadership development program]

Format the output as Markdown. Start with a persona snapshot, then the structured sections.

**Persona snapshot**
- Name: [a grounded first + last name — plausible, not theatrical]
- Role: [specific title + context, e.g., "Senior Operations Manager, Store District 7" — not just "Manager"]
- Headline: [one sentence that captures who they are]
- Story: [3–5 sentences. A slice of their working life showing how they experience the gap this training is meant to address. Specific, grounded, no backstory padding.]

Then:

1. **Identity & context** — Role, scope of work, where they sit in the org, what their day looks like. Short — table-setting, not the persona.

2. **What they care about** — What motivates them. What they're trying to prove or protect. What their boss judges them on.

3. **What they resist** — What they push back on. What they've seen too many times. What feels condescending. What they consider beneath them.

4. **The lived gap** — How they currently experience the problem this training is supposed to address. What they're currently doing (or not doing). Whether they even agree there's a gap.

5. **Environment** — Where and when they'll engage with this learning. Devices, time pressure, distractions, manager support. Whether the conditions exist to apply what they learn.

6. **Design implications** — Given everything above, what does this persona say about: modality, length, tone, examples, scenarios, assessment style. Be specific.

Rules:
- Every claim should be grounded in the inputs OR explicitly flagged as inference. Use "INFERRED — verify" for guesses.
- Don't pad. If the inputs don't say enough to populate a section confidently, write "thin — needs more discovery" instead of inventing.
- The Design Implications section is non-negotiable. If a persona doesn't drive design decisions, it's not earning its keep.
- Give them a name and a grounded story. Make them feel like a real person whose calendar you could picture — not a stock character. No backstories, no quirks for quirks' sake — just specificity.

Don't:
- Default to demographic descriptions ("mid-career professional, 35–50") — those tell us nothing.
- Hedge with "they might" or "they could" — pick a direction grounded in evidence or flag the uncertainty.
- Cover everyone in the audience. A useful persona is specific to one core type. If multi-audience, build separate personas.
prompt_fast.tune: If the inputs contradict each other (e.g., one source says learners are highly motivated, another says they resist training), surface that contradiction in the Lived Gap section as a tension to resolve, not a guess at which is right.

pro_tip: Ask AI to build it in the voice of a real housewife. You will get a draft that you can have fun with, for a minute. Just don't submit it to your client!

title: Build knowledge checks at the right cognitive level
teaser: Most knowledge checks ask the wrong question.
intro: Knowledge checks are only useful if they measure what your learning outcome promises. If the outcome says "apply" and the check asks for a definition, you're measuring at the wrong cognitive level. You end up with high pass rates and no behavior change.
how_ai_helps: Give AI the learning outcome with its Bloom verb (remember, understand, apply, analyze, evaluate, create), plus the audience and the source content. It can draft checks at the matching cognitive level so that not every question is "What is… X."
ai_wont: AI doesn't know if your distractors in the answers are plausible. A question with three obviously-wrong answers and one obviously-right one doesn't measure anything.
steps: Have your learning outcome ready with the Bloom verb visible (if it says "understand," stop — that's not a Bloom verb). Gather the source content the check relates to. Paste both into the prompt and run.
Review the distractors. AI's first pass usually has at least one too-easy distractor. Tell it which one is too obvious and ask for a replacement that represents a real misconception in your audience.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior instructional designer who writes assessments that actually match the cognitive level of the learning outcome. You don't default to recall.
prompt_fast.ask: Draft knowledge checks for the learning outcome below, at the cognitive level specified.
prompt_fast.shape:
Source content: [PASTE the section of the module or source material this check relates to. If running from your AI workspace where source is already loaded, write "see workspace."]
Learning outcome: [PASTE — include the Bloom verb]
Bloom level: [remember / understand / apply / analyze / evaluate / create]
Audience: [WHO, ROLE, WHAT THEY ALREADY KNOW]
Question format: [e.g., multiple choice, scenario, drag-and-drop, short answer]
How many checks I need: [3-5]

Format the output as Markdown. For each check, include:
- The question
- The answer choices (with the correct one clearly marked)
- A one-line rationale for why each distractor represents a plausible misconception, not a strawman
- A note on which Bloom level the check actually operates at and why

Rules:
- If the outcome is at "apply" or higher, do NOT write a recall question. The default temptation is to drop down to recall — resist it.
- Distractors must be plausible. No "obviously wrong to anyone paying attention" options.
- Use language the audience would actually use, not textbook phrasing.

Don't:
- Pad with extra questions to hit a round number.
- Write a question where two of the answer choices could both be correct.
- Use trick questions or "all of the above / none of the above" unless the outcome specifically calls for that judgment.
prompt_fast.tune: If the learning outcome is fuzzy ("understand X"), surface that as a problem — fuzzy outcomes can't be assessed. Ask me to sharpen the verb before drafting. If you genuinely can't write a non-recall check for what I've given you, say so and explain why.

pro_tip: After AI drafts the checks, run a quick test on each one: cover the answer choices, read the question, and try to answer it yourself in your own words. If your answer matches one of the choices, the question is well-written. If your answer isn't there or there's ambiguity, the check needs work.

title: Choose the right modality
teaser: Sometimes the answer isn't a course.
intro:
Whenever I pack for my vacations, I create a list based on what I expect my vacation will be. I'm basically designing a wardrobe by making assumptions of what I will need based on the number of days, the weather, the activities I will be doing. I actually use AI to help me create that list by asking questions to me, so that its recommendation is data-driven.

When designing a strategy for training, you can also use AI to help you think through those decisions — for example, what modality to use.
how_ai_helps: Tell AI the topic, the audience, and the modality you're already leaning toward. Instead of recommending a format right away, AI will ask the questions a senior PM would — why this format, what you're assuming about the audience, what could go wrong if you committed.
ai_wont: AI doesn't know your audience's actual constraints — whether they have laptops, whether they're on the road, whether their managers care if they finish.
steps: Start with your hunch. What modality are you already leaning toward, and why? If you can't name it, go back to the needs assessment first.
Walk through the prompt as a conversation. Answer honestly — including the awkward "I don't know yet."
Capture what you decided AND why. Modality decisions get revisited mid-project, and future-you will want the receipts.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior instructional designer who has watched a lot of projects pick the wrong modality and pay for it. You're known for asking sharp questions before offering opinions.
prompt_fast.ask: Help me think through which modality fits this learning need. Do NOT recommend anything yet. Ask me questions first.
prompt_fast.shape:
Topic: [WHAT'S THE LEARNING NEED]
Audience: [WHO, ROLE, WHERE THEY WORK, AVAILABLE TIME]
What I'm currently leaning toward: [BE HONEST — even if it's "slides because that's what we always do"]
What I know about constraints: [BUDGET, TIMELINE, TOOLS AVAILABLE, ANYTHING ELSE]

Rules of engagement:
- Ask me one question at a time. Don't dump a questionnaire.
- Start by interrogating my current leaning. Why am I considering it? What evidence supports it?
- Surface my assumptions. If I'm assuming something about the audience, the constraints, or the goal, call it out.
- Push back when I'm being reflexive. If I say "we always do slides," ask why.
- Map tradeoffs across at least three realistic modalities for THIS situation — not in the abstract.
- Ask the disqualifying questions: "If you committed to X, what could go wrong?"
- Don't recommend until you've stress-tested the leading option AND at least two alternatives.
- When you do offer a recommendation, frame it as "Based on what you've told me, here's the tradeoff to weigh — you decide."
prompt_fast.tune: If I give you thin input, ask for more rather than guessing. If my answers contradict each other (e.g., "audience has no time" plus "I want a 2-hour workshop"), surface the tension as a question. Treat "because we always do it that way" as a non-answer that deserves a follow-up.

pro_tip: If AI keeps confirming whatever you said you wanted to do, it's being too agreeable. Tighten the Frame to push back harder — try "You are a senior ID who has seen too many bad modality picks. Push back on my reasoning." A good session ends with you saying "I wasn't going to do that, but actually it makes sense."

title: Audit a module against your style guide
teaser: Did you actually follow the guide you wrote?
intro: A style guide is only useful if you actually follow it. After a couple of long design sessions, it's likely that you'll slip. This is where AI can help you be an objective auditor of your work. It's one of those AI use cases that I find helps me focus on creative work, while they focus on the more "tedious" consistency check.
how_ai_helps: AI can read the module and the style guide together and tell you, line by line, where you diverged. Not in a generic "this could be clearer" way — specifically: your style guide says use "learner," you used "student" in modules 2, 4, and 5.
ai_wont: AI is good at line-level issues — vocabulary, naming, banned phrases, structural rules. It's worse at judgment calls — "is this voice actually warm enough?" or "would your audience read this as condescending?" Treat AI as your consistency checker. You're still the voice critic.
steps: Have your draft and your style guide ready. Paste both into the prompt — or run this from inside your AI workspace where both files already live (workspace card listed below).

Triage the findings. Some are mechanical — just fix. Some are arguable — you broke the rule on purpose. Some are the guide being wrong — update the guide if the call you made is actually better than the rule.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior instructional designer doing a careful line-level audit of a module against its style guide.
prompt_fast.ask: Audit the module below against the style guide. Find every place where the module diverges from the guide.
prompt_fast.shape:
Style guide: [PASTE OR REFERENCE]
Module draft: [PASTE OR REFERENCE]

Format the output as Markdown with these four sections:

1. **Voice & tone violations** — Where the module's voice diverges from the guide. Quote the line in the module. Quote the rule in the guide. Suggest a fix.

2. **Vocabulary & terminology** — Banned words used, defined-on-first-use terms left undefined, inconsistent terminology. Specific instances only.

3. **Structural & convention violations** — Naming conventions, formatting, length, anything the guide laid out as a rule.

4. **Ambiguous cases** — Where the module is arguably fine but you're not sure. Flag for me to decide.

Don't:
- Pad with generic writing advice ('be clearer', 'use active voice') unless the guide actually says that.
- Flag stylistic choices the guide doesn't speak to.
- Skim. Cite the line in the module AND the rule in the guide for every finding.
prompt_fast.tune: If the style guide is silent on something the module does, say so explicitly — that's a guide gap, not a module violation. If you're not confident in a finding, mark it as "low confidence" instead of dropping it.

pro_tip: Run this audit halfway through the build, not just at the end. Catching three voice drifts at module 3 is much easier than catching twelve at module 9.

title: Set up your AI workspace
teaser: The onboarding pack for your AI project.
intro:
AI tools today let you pre-load reference material and instructions so every chat starts smarter. For training development this is especially useful — you load the template, the style guide, a brief of context, and you stop providing it again every time you open a new chat.

Each LLM has its own version of this — Claude Project, Custom GPT, Gemini Gems — but ultimately, it's a workspace. When you need a consistent answer based on a set of sources, this is the way to go.

Here are the instructions for the three most common tools.

Go to chatgpt.com → Explore GPTs → Create. Use the GPT Builder to set instructions, name it, and upload your reference files under Knowledge. Save.

Read OpenAI's official guide↗

Go to gemini.google.com → Explore Gems → New Gem. Name it, write the instructions, and attach your reference files. Save.

Read Google's official guide↗
pro_tip: Update the files in the workspace whenever your design decisions change — style guide update, refined persona, anything.

title: Prep for a needs assessment call
teaser: Because "good conversation" is not a deliverable.
intro:
I've done more than 40 escape rooms. Every single time, during the first couple of minutes I stand there and take it all in — patterns on the walls, props on the table, the things that look important and (often more useful) the things that aren't there.

A needs assessment is the same. Your first job isn't just to ask your questions. It's to notice what's in the room — what they care about, what they keep coming back to, and what they're carefully not saying.
how_ai_helps: AI is good at making you smarter before the meeting so you can walk in better prepared. It's great at surfacing questions you didn't know you needed answered.
ai_wont: AI doesn't know your stakeholder personally, and it can't tell you what's politically sensitive in your org. Try to anticipate that one yourself (if you can).
steps: Pull together everything you already know about the project and the person. Old emails, the meeting invite, the kickoff doc, the org chart — whatever's lying around. The more you give the prompt, the less it has to guess.
Read the output as a draft, not a script. Cut what feels generic. Sharpen what's specific to your situation.
Use section 2 ("what you need to walk out with") as your meeting compass. If the conversation drifts, you have the questions to steer it back.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior instructional designer who has run hundreds of needs assessment calls.
prompt_fast.ask: Prep me for the needs assessment call described below.
prompt_fast.shape:
Project context: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE — kickoff brief, project doc, prior emails, anything I've got. Messy is fine.]
Who I'm meeting with: [NAME, ROLE, what they care about, any prior interactions]
How much time we have: [e.g., 30 min, 1 hour]

Format the output as Markdown with these five sections:

1. **Context & stakeholder read** — A 5-bullet brief on the project state and what's likely on this person's mind. What they care about, what they're under pressure on.

2. **What I need to walk out with** — The concrete inputs this call has to produce: the gap, the audience, the constraints, the success criteria, what's been tried. Be specific to THIS project.

3. **Questions — both sides of the table** —
   - Questions to ask them. Each one tied to a specific item in section 2. No floating questions.
   - Questions they're likely to ask me. With a sentence of draft answer.

4. **Glossary** — Acronyms, internal product names, or domain terms from the input. Brief definitions so I don't nod blankly.

5. **If we only have 15 minutes** — The 2–3 questions I cannot leave without asking.

Don't:
- Ask questions that aren't tied to a takeaway in section 2.
- Invent context about the stakeholder. If you're inferring, flag it.
prompt_fast.tune: Where you had to guess at what this person cares about because I didn't give you enough input, call it out — those are sanity-check items, not facts. If my context doesn't match a needs assessment (e.g., we're already past discovery), flag it before answering.

pro_tip: Take the takeaways list from section 2 and turn it into a short paragraph you send the stakeholder 24 hours before the meeting: "Here's where we are, here's what I'd love to get out of our conversation, here are two questions I'd appreciate you thinking about beforehand."

title: Build a Style Guide for your project
teaser: So your fourth course doesn't sound like a different person wrote it.
intro:
On my first project as a lead, I was coordinating a couple of instructional designers based out of Argentina and India. Development happened in shifts — Argentina would hand over to India, and the next day Argentina would get the work back. So we'd wake up to consistency and style issues. One course called the audience "learners." Another said "associates." A third went with "users."

This was because there was no single source of truth for designers to consult. We needed the bible for every instructional designer: a style guide.
how_ai_helps: Ask AI to draft the style guide including voice, vocabulary, and format decisions upfront.
ai_wont: AI doesn't know your brand. You'll need to provide inputs and mark up the first draft.
steps: Gather inputs: brand guidelines (if any), one piece of content that sounds the way you want this to sound, audience, modalities you'll produce.
Use the prompt and edit the output. Cut what's generic. Sharpen what's specific.
Validate with your stakeholders.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior learning experience designer who has written style guides for dozens of projects across industries.
prompt_fast.ask: Draft a style guide for the project described below.
prompt_fast.shape:
Project topic: [TOPIC]
Audience: [WHO — role, expertise, motivation, context they'll read in]
Modalities you'll produce: [e.g., e-learning + job aids + facilitator notes]
Existing brand guidelines: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE — voice attributes, banned words, tone rules, visual basics. If none yet, say "none yet."]
Sample content (optional, strong signal): [PASTE one piece you'd want this project to sound like]
Audience constraints (optional): [reading level, accessibility, languages, cultural considerations]

Format the output as Markdown so I can paste it into other prompts later.

Sections to include — but ONLY if I've given you input to ground them:
- Voice & tone (3–5 voice attributes; each with do/don't + one example)
- Vocabulary (terms to use, terms to avoid, terms to define on first use)
- Examples & analogies (what kinds work for THIS audience, what to avoid)
- Naming conventions (modules, characters, sections, files)
- Modality-specific rules (different per modality — slide rules ≠ video rules)
- Accessibility & inclusive language (concrete dos and don'ts for THIS audience)
- Visual treatment (only if visual specs were provided; otherwise skip)

Also add any other sections the input suggests are needed. If the source uses emoji, include a section on emoji. If it references regulated terms, include a section on regulated language. Use your judgment.

For every rule, include a concrete example. No generic advice.

Don't:
- Pad with sections I didn't ground in input.
- Use generic style-guide examples. Every example should be plausible for THIS project, with THIS audience, in THIS voice.
- Add "be authentic" or "be human" or "write clearly" without a concrete behavioral example.
prompt_fast.tune: Where you had to guess at the brand voice because I didn't give you enough input, flag it explicitly under each section. If the brand guidelines and the audience pull in opposite directions (e.g., very formal brand, very young audience), surface the tension as a question for me to answer, not a guess. If a section can't be grounded in input, skip it instead of padding.

pro_tip: Drop the final version into a Claude Project or Custom GPT. Every authoring prompt in that workspace starts with the style guide loaded.

title: Turn a topic into a module outline
teaser: Because "I'll just start with the slides" never ends well.
intro:
Have you ever assembled an IKEA piece without reading the instructions? You're three steps in, you've already used the wrong screws, and there's a piece called "BESPIK" that doesn't fit anywhere.

When creating a course, the first thing you should do is draft an outline. It will help you visualize where you want to get to so you don't have to figure it out along the way.
how_ai_helps: Feed AI the topic, audience, and any source material. It'll draft a first-pass outline — section names, content to include in each, and a quick reason for each section's position. The prompt below caps the section count, so you get something tight instead of bloated.
ai_wont: AI doesn't know your learners. After you have an outline, you still need to check it against what you learned in Analysis and Discovery.
steps: Gather your inputs — topic, audience, and any source bullet points or documents. The more you give the prompt, the better the first draft. Paste into the prompt and run.
Iterate. Ask for a different sequence, a different section count, a different progression. The first output is rarely the final outline.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior instructional designer outlining the content structure of a single course or module.
prompt_fast.ask: Draft a section-by-section outline for the topic below.
prompt_fast.shape:
Topic: [TOPIC]
Audience: [WHO THE LEARNERS ARE — role, expertise level, motivation]
Format: [e.g., 60-minute live workshop / 4-hour async / 30-min microlearning]
Source material (optional): [BULLET POINTS OR PASTED SOURCE CONTENT]

Rules:
- 4 to 6 sections. Don't go over.
- Each section needs: a title, 3–5 sub-points covering the content, and a one-line rationale for why it sits in this position.
- Sequence the sections so each one enables the next.

Don't:
- Pad with filler sections to hit a number.
- Use generic section names like "Introduction" or "Conclusion" without specific content tied to the topic.
- Number sections 1–N unless the order actually matters.
- Include learning objectives — that's the curriculum's job, not the outline's.
prompt_fast.tune: Where you assumed something, call it out — those become my SME questions. If the brief is too thin to design from, ask me one clarifying question first instead of guessing.

pro_tip: If you have actual source material — SOPs, interview transcripts, articles — drop it into a NotebookLM notebook and run this prompt in there. The outline comes back grounded in your real sources, with citations. Way less hallucinated content, and much easier to defend to an SME.
